message.content: Read all memos concerning windows access.  I am wondering if we can attempt a run of the gauntlet under wsl or cygwin now.   I think WSL; I vaguely recall we ran into a cygwin blocker.  Orient yourself.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Let me read the Windows memos, starting with the most recent and relevant ones.

content.content:
1	# Windows Substrate Landscape for Theurge
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-17
4	
5	## Why this memo exists
6	
7	Recipe Bottle's gauntlet — and even the smallest fast-tier test suite —
8	runs through the theurge Rust binary, which spawns bash sub-processes
9	and embeds filesystem paths in their scripts. That makes theurge the
10	substrate-sensitive piece of the Recipe Bottle stack: it has to agree
11	with its surrounding shell about what a path looks like.
12	
13	On a Windows host, there are two readily-available shell substrates that
14	*both* present a bash-ish interface — Cygwin and WSL2. They are not
15	interchangeable from theurge's perspective. This memo records what we
16	observed from each, without picking a winner. The cost ledger that
17	determines which substrate is right for a given operator includes
18	factors outside the substrate itself (multi-distro coexistence with
19	Docker Desktop's WSL machinery, account topology, garrison roadmap)
20	that this memo does not attempt to weigh.
21	
22	The investigation was prompted by a first-time bring-up of Recipe Bottle
23	on a fresh Cygwin account (`brad@rocket`) where `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`
24	aborted in `enrollment-validation` with a substrate-level path-mangling
25	failure. Both substrates were probed in the same session.
26	
27	## The Cygwin substrate, today
28	
29	### Observed failure mode
30	
31	After installing rustup as `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` and building theurge
32	cleanly under a Cygwin shell, the gauntlet aborted on its first fixture
33	(`enrollment-validation`) with `0 passed, 1 failed (1 total)` — but the
34	fast suite normally runs 47 cases in that fixture alone. The Rust
35	binary's reported trace directory was
36	
37	```
38	Trace dir: /cygdrive/c/Users/<user>/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/../temp-buk/temp-<stamp>\rbtd
39	```
40	
41	— note the mixed `/` and `\` separators in a single path. The on-disk
42	state varied between runs:
43	
44	- One run produced a *sibling* directory `temp-<stamp>rbtd` (no
45	  separator between the timestamp and `rbtd`), parallel to the expected
46	  `temp-<stamp>` rather than nested inside it.
47	- A subsequent run produced no `*rbtd*` directory anywhere visible to
48	  Cygwin — the `create_dir_all` call did not raise an error to the
49	  Rust binary, but the directory was not present.
50	
51	`ls -b` confirmed the sibling-directory name contained no literal
52	backslash character; Windows had elided the `\` separator entirely
53	during path normalization of the mixed-slash input.
54	
55	### Root cause shape
56	
57	The Rust source is structurally correct: `main.rs` uses
58	`burd_temp.join("rbtd")` and `rbtdre_engine.rs` uses
59	`root_temp.join(case.name)`. There is no string-concatenation bug. The
60	failure occurs at a *substrate boundary*: a Windows-native binary
61	consuming Cygwin POSIX paths (`/cygdrive/c/...`) and combining them
62	with Windows-native separators via `PathBuf::join`. Windows path
63	normalization of the result is implementation-defined for these inputs,
64	which is why behavior varies between runs.
65	
66	### Three boundary classes (scope estimate if revisited)
67	
68	For a future attempt at making theurge work under Cygwin without
69	abandoning the Windows-native target, three boundary classes need
70	systematic handling:
71	
72	1. **Env-var intake from Cygwin bash → Rust.** Single chokepoint:
73	   `rbtdb_read_dispatch_dir` in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` reads
74	   `BURD_TEMP_DIR`, `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`, etc. as POSIX strings. A
75	   conversion helper (e.g., `cygpath -w` equivalent) would convert
76	   incoming paths to Windows-native `PathBuf` at this single site.
77	2. **Script-embedded paths from Rust → Cygwin bash.** The chokepoint
78	   is `rbtdrf_run_bash` in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` (called
79	   ~11 times in that file alone), but the *interpolation sites* are
80	   the callers that build the script string via
81	   `format!("source '{}'", path.display())`. Every such site needs to
82	   replace `path.display()` with a POSIX-form conversion. Discovery
83	   recipe: `grep -rn "source '{}'" Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` plus adjacent
84	   `format!()` blocks. ~9-15 sites in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`; the
85	   `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` sibling may have more.
86	3. **Env vars on tabtarget spawn.** `Command::new(&tt)` sites in
87	   `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, `rbtdrf_handbook.rs`, `rbtdri_invocation.rs`
88	   inherit the parent's env. If intake conversion (boundary 1) is
89	   applied, env vars need re-conversion back to POSIX before any
90	   tabtarget spawn, since tabtargets re-enter `bud_dispatch` and read
91	   POSIX paths.
92	
93	Windows-native tool spawns (`Command::new("git")`, `"curl"`,
94	`"docker"`) need no conversion — Windows-form paths are what those
95	tools natively want.
96	
97	### Unknown tail
98	
99	Even with all three boundaries handled, BUK-side bash itself may have
100	Cygwin assumptions that aren't yet visible. `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` slash
101	trimming, `realpath` semantics, `readlink -f` behavior, and similar
102	patterns could surface as the next blocker. The mechanical theurge fix
103	unlocks visibility into the next layer; whether that layer is clean is
104	empirically unknown.
105	
106	## Cygwin substrate, revisited 2026-05-19
107	
108	Pace ₢BOAAM (path-transmute) landed at commit `ea2d04b` and was
109	verified on `cygwin@rocket`. The path-transmutation code itself is
110	correct, but two BUK-side / theurge-invocation blockers surface
111	behind it. This section retires the "Unknown tail" prediction with
112	empirical content.
113	
114	### Path-transmutation is correct but doesn't engage by default
115	
116	`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrx_platform.rs:45` keys Cygwin detection on
117	`std::env::var("OSTYPE").as_deref() == Ok("cygwin")`. The module
118	docstring (line 42) asserts *"the launching bash exports
119	`OSTYPE=cygwin`"* — empirically false. Bash sets `OSTYPE` as a
120	non-exported shell variable; `env | grep ^OSTYPE=` from a Cygwin
121	session returns nothing, and the Windows-native `rbtd.exe` spawned
122	by a tabtarget sees no such env var. `rbtdrx_is_cygwin()` returns
123	false, so every intake/outflow site in the module operates as
124	identity. The mixed-separator failure described in "Observed
125	failure mode" above reproduces unchanged on the landed code.
126	
127	Forcing `export OSTYPE` in the parent shell before invoking
128	`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` exercises the transmutation. Same
129	suite, two runs:
130	
131	```
132	default:        Trace dir: /cygdrive/c/Users/.../temp-...\rbtd
133	                                      (mixed-slash, no rbtd/ created)
134	export OSTYPE:  Trace dir: C:\Users\cygwin\projects\...\temp-...\rbtd
135	                                      (clean native, rbtd/ exists)
136	```
137	
138	`grep -rn OSTYPE Tools/buk Tools/rbk/rbtd` finds zero export sites
139	in the launcher chain. Either the BUK launcher needs to learn
140	`export OSTYPE`, or `rbtdrx_is_cygwin()` needs a detection signal
141	that does propagate to child processes (e.g., `cygpath --version`
142	succeeding, or shelling out to `uname -o` returning `Cygwin`).
143	
144	### `Command::new("bash")` finds Windows WSL stub, not Cygwin bash
145	
146	With OSTYPE forcibly exported, path-transmutation produces clean
147	native trace paths and the per-case directory is created. The
148	first case (`rbtdrf_ev_string_valid`) then fails with UTF-16LE
149	stderr from `wsl.exe`:
150	
151	```
152	Windows Subsystem for Linux has no installed distributions.
153	```
154	
155	`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs:68` calls
156	`Command::new("bash").arg("-c").arg(script)`. From a Windows-
157	native process, `CreateProcessW` uses the standard search order:
158	application directory, current directory, *system directory
159	(`C:\Windows\System32`)*, Windows directory, then `PATH`.
160	System32 ships `bash.exe` — the WSL launcher stub installed by
161	default on Windows 10+. Cygwin's `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe`
162	appears later via `PATH` and is never reached.
163	
164	`cmd.exe /c where bash` from a Cygwin shell returns
165	`C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe` first because `where` performs a
166	`PATH`-only lookup; CreateProcessW's broader search is what trips
167	the Rust call. Reproduced with a 10-line standalone Rust binary
168	independent of theurge.
169	
170	Fix path (out of scope for ₢BOAAM): resolve the bash absolute
171	path once at startup (e.g., via `cygpath -w /usr/bin/bash` →
172	`C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe`) and pass it to `Command::new`, rather
173	than relying on lookup order. Affects `rbtdrf_run_bash` and any
174	sibling site that spawns `bash` (or other Cygwin utilities whose
175	name collides with a System32 binary).
176	
177	### Done condition for ₢BOAAM, literal vs effective
178	
179	The pace's Done condition was *"`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh`
180	reaches a verdict on Cygwin."* Literally: met — the binary
181	terminates with exit 1 and emits `0 passed, 1 failed (1 total)`.
182	Effectively: the suite exercises only 1 of 75 cases before the
183	bash-lookup blocker stops it, which is not a transmutation gap.
184	The transmutation code does what it claims when its detection
185	signal is present.
186	
187	### rustup install prerequisites on Cygwin
188	
189	Standing up a fresh `cygwin@rocket` → x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
190	rustup install. Three gotchas not in the rustup docs:
191	
192	1. **No MinGW system install needed.** Rust 1.95.0's
193	   `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain ships a self-contained
194	   linker. `cargo build` on theurge linked clean without
195	   `mingw64-x86_64-gcc-core` or any `setup-x86_64.exe -P`
196	   step. This eliminates the elevation barrier — the cygwin
197	   user does not need admin to install rust.
198	
199	2. **`TMPDIR` must be a Windows-form path.** PATH on rocket
200	   resolves `curl` to `C:\WINDOWS\system32\curl.exe` (Schannel-
201	   backed Windows curl), not Cygwin's curl. rustup-init.sh's
202	   bootstrap downloader hands curl a Cygwin POSIX `/tmp/...`
203	   destination; Windows curl writes ~5KB then fails with
204	   `curl: (23) client returned ERROR on write` (curl error 23
205	   is "write failed"). Workaround:
206	
207	   ```
208	   mkdir -p ~/rust-install-tmp
209	   export TMPDIR="$(cygpath -w ~/rust-install-tmp)"
210	   sh rustup-init.sh -y --default-toolchain stable \
211	       --profile minimal --no-modify-path
212	   ```
213	
214	3. **Do not pass `--default-host`.** The rustup-init.sh wrapper
215	   detects Cygwin via uname and inserts its own
216	   `--default-host x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` before invoking the
217	   downloaded rustup-init.exe. Passing the flag again from the
218	   command line produces:
219	
220	   ```
221	   error: the argument '--default-host <DEFAULT_HOST>' cannot
222	   be used multiple times
223	   ```
224	
225	   Let the wrapper auto-detect; the installer line
226	   `info: setting default host triple to x86_64-pc-windows-gnu`
227	   confirms the correct host was chosen.
228	
229	### SSH transport detail for cygwin@rocket
230	
231	`memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`'s
232	noninteractive forced-command for `cygwin@rocket` is
233	`bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. Empirically, argv-mode
234	commands containing `$var` references arrive at the remote bash
235	without parameter expansion:
236	
237	```
238	$ ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo $OSTYPE'
239	$OSTYPE                                  # literal, not 'cygwin'
240	$ printf '%s\n' 'echo $OSTYPE' | ssh cygwin@rocket bash -l
241	cygwin                                   # correct
242	```
243	
244	Cause unconfirmed (the forced-command's shell quoting interacts
245	with how sshd reconstructs `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` through Windows
246	OpenSSH's argv-to-string join); the empirical pattern is
247	reliable. For noninteractive automation against `cygwin@rocket`,
248	use the stdin-pipe form. Argv-mode is acceptable for commands
249	that contain no `$` expansion and no operator chaining.
250	
251	## The WSL substrate, today
252	
253	### Observed result
254	
255	A fresh Ubuntu-24.04 distro provisioned on the same Windows host, with
256	rustup installed and the repo cloned into ext4, ran the fast suite as
257	follows:
258	
259	- Theurge built cleanly (`Finished dev profile target(s) in 4.27s`).
260	- `enrollment-validation`: **47/47 passed** — the exact fixture that
261	  could not complete a single case on Cygwin.
262	- `regime-validation`: 24/25 passed. The one failure
263	  (`rbtdrf_rv_rbrr_repo`) emitted
264	  `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX: RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX must not be empty` — a
265	  config-gap in the clone-of-clone chain (Mac → fresh Cygwin checkout
266	  → WSL clone) that would surface identically on any never-configured
267	  checkout regardless of OS.
268	
269	Substrate-attributable cases: 71/71 passed. No mixed-separator
270	artifacts; trace directories landed where the Rust binary said they
271	would.
272	
273	### Setup prerequisites observed
274	
275	- `wsl --install -d Ubuntu-24.04 --no-launch` — see operational traps
276	  below; `--no-launch` is load-bearing for remote invocation.
277	- `apt-get install -y build-essential` — rustup warns "no default
278	  linker (`cc`) was found in your PATH" after a minimal-profile
279	  install; theurge builds C-using crates (e.g., libc build script,
280	  typenum, zerocopy) and fails to link without `cc`.
281	- A station regime file at `../station-files/burs.env` — BUK's
282	  first-run gate. The error message enumerates the three required
283	  variables (`BURS_LOG_DIR`, `BURS_USER`, `BURS_TINCTURE`).
284	
285	## Operational traps when driving WSL from another machine
286	
287	These applied when driving the Windows host from a Mac through the
288	existing `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh` chain (Mac bash → ssh →
289	Windows OpenSSH → cmd.exe → PowerShell → wsl.exe → bash). They are
290	specific to that transit, not to WSL itself.
291	
292	### `wsl --install` over SSH
293	
294	A bare `wsl --install -d <distro>` from a privileged SSH session
295	fails with
296	
297	```
298	The requested operation requires elevation.
299	This operation requires an interactive window station.
300	Error code: Wsl/0x800705b3
301	```
302	
303	The elevation part is solved by being on the privileged SSH path. The
304	window-station part is the post-install distro launch, which wants a
305	console for the first-time user-creation prompt. **Fix**: append
306	`--no-launch`. The distro installs successfully and remains in
307	"Stopped" state with `root` as the only account.
308	
309	### `wsl.exe` UTF-16LE output
310	
311	`wsl -l -v` (and `wsl --status` and friends) emit UTF-16LE directly.
312	Through cmd-mode SSH transport, the interleaved null bytes terminate
313	C-string handling and the output appears empty. **Fix**: invoke
314	through a PowerShell wrapper (PowerShell handles UTF-16 natively as
315	its console encoding) or set `WSL_UTF8=1` in the environment before
316	invoking wsl.
317	
318	### PowerShell 5.1 quote-stripping
319	
320	```
321	powershell -Command 'wsl -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "cd /path && tt/foo.sh"'
322	```
323	
324	fails with
325	
326	```
327	The token '&&' is not a valid statement separator in this version.
328	```
329	
330	The cmd.exe → PowerShell argv shuffle strips the inner double quotes
331	around the `bash -lc` argument. PowerShell then receives the bash
332	command unquoted, parses `&&` as a PowerShell operator, and rejects it
333	(PS 5.1 has no `&&`; PS 7+ has it as a pipeline chain operator,
334	which still doesn't match the bash semantic we wanted). **Fix**: use
335	`wsl --cd <dir>` to set the working directory natively in WSL, then
336	invoke a single command without a `cd && ...` chain:
337	
338	```
339	powershell -Command 'wsl -d <distro> -u root --cd /root/proj -- bash -lc tt/foo.sh'
340	```
341	
342	### `/mnt/c` local-clone
343	
344	The Windows filesystem is mounted at `/mnt/c/` inside WSL. Cygwin's
345	existing checkout at `/cygdrive/c/Users/<user>/projects/<repo>` is
346	therefore reachable from WSL as
347	`/mnt/c/Users/<user>/projects/<repo>`. Running tabtargets directly
348	from that mount surfaces FS-metadata problems (executable bits,
349	filename case, performance under high-fanout test fixtures).
350	**Workaround**:
351	
352	```
353	wsl -d <distro> -u root -- git clone /mnt/c/Users/<user>/projects/<repo> /root/projects/<repo>
354	```
355	
356	A local clone copies into ext4 cleanly, and tests run from the WSL
357	home directory rather than the Windows mount. The same trick lets you
358	copy single files across (e.g., `cp /mnt/c/.../station-files/burs.env
359	/root/.../station-files/burs.env`) without needing a separate
360	network transport.
361	
362	## Live work captured in ₣BO
363	
364	Two paces in heat ₣BO carry the substrate work forward:
365	
366	- **₢BOAAM (cygwin-theurge-path-transmute)** — landed at commit
367	  `ea2d04b` on 2026-05-19, verified on `cygwin@rocket`. The
368	  path-transmutation code itself is correct, but two follow-on
369	  BUK-side / theurge-invocation blockers surface behind it (OSTYPE
370	  not exported by bash so the detection signal never reaches the
371	  Windows-native binary; `Command::new("bash")` resolves to
372	  System32's WSL stub before Cygwin's bash via CreateProcessW
373	  search order). Detail in the "Cygwin substrate, revisited
374	  2026-05-19" section above.
375	- **₢BOAAN (cygwin-wsl-noninteractive-ssh-personas)** — set up
376	  `cygwin@rocket` and `wsl@rocket` as parallel headless SSH accounts,
377	  each forced-command'd to one substrate. Independent of the
378	  substrate-viability question; useful for either substrate, since it
379	  collapses the multi-step transit chain in this memo's "Operational
380	  traps" section to a single `ssh wsl@rocket <cmd>` or
381	  `ssh cygwin@rocket <cmd>` invocation.
382	
383	Both paces remain live. This memo records what we know, not which
384	substrate to invest in next.
385	
386	## See also
387	
388	- `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` — SSH
389	  account auth chain (recipe for both `cygwin@rocket` and
390	  `wsl@rocket` setup); the "Noninteractive forced-command variants"
391	  section covers the noninteractive personas and the WSL per-user
392	  registration finding (`wsl --install` needs admin; `wsl --import`
393	  does not)
394	- `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` and the
395	  `memo-2026051*-windows-transport-*` series — broader empirical
396	  record of cmd.exe / PowerShell / wsl.exe transport quoting; the
397	  trap in this memo's PowerShell-quote-stripping section is one
398	  instance of that family
399	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md` — Windows
400	  scripting guide; the codified body-authoring rules for transit
401	  through cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell stacks
402	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` and `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`
403	  — chokepoints for the three boundary classes in the Cygwin section
404	- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` — `bujb_privileged_ssh` (the
405	  underpinning of `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh`)
406

# Windows Substrate Landscape for Theurge

Date: 2026-05-17

## Why this memo exists

Recipe Bottle's gauntlet — and even the smallest fast-tier test suite —
runs through the theurge Rust binary, which spawns bash sub-processes
and embeds filesystem paths in their scripts. That makes theurge the
substrate-sensitive piece of the Recipe Bottle stack: it has to agree
with its surrounding shell about what a path looks like.

On a Windows host, there are two readily-available shell substrates that
*both* present a bash-ish interface — Cygwin and WSL2. They are not
interchangeable from theurge's perspective. This memo records what we
observed from each, without picking a winner. The cost ledger that
determines which substrate is right for a given operator includes
factors outside the substrate itself (multi-distro coexistence with
Docker Desktop's WSL machinery, account topology, garrison roadmap)
that this memo does not attempt to weigh.

The investigation was prompted by a first-time bring-up of Recipe Bottle
on a fresh Cygwin account (`brad@rocket`) where `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`
aborted in `enrollment-validation` with a substrate-level path-mangling
failure. Both substrates were probed in the same session.

## The Cygwin substrate, today

### Observed failure mode

After installing rustup as `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` and building theurge
cleanly under a Cygwin shell, the gauntlet aborted on its first fixture
(`enrollment-validation`) with `0 passed, 1 failed (1 total)` — but the
fast suite normally runs 47 cases in that fixture alone. The Rust
binary's reported trace directory was

```
Trace dir: /cygdrive/c/Users/<user>/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/../temp-buk/temp-<stamp>\rbtd
```

— note the mixed `/` and `\` separators in a single path. The on-disk
state varied between runs:

- One run produced a *sibling* directory `temp-<stamp>rbtd` (no
  separator between the timestamp and `rbtd`), parallel to the expected
  `temp-<stamp>` rather than nested inside it.
- A subsequent run produced no `*rbtd*` directory anywhere visible to
  Cygwin — the `create_dir_all` call did not raise an error to the
  Rust binary, but the directory was not present.

`ls -b` confirmed the sibling-directory name contained no literal
backslash character; Windows had elided the `\` separator entirely
during path normalization of the mixed-slash input.

### Root cause shape

The Rust source is structurally correct: `main.rs` uses
`burd_temp.join("rbtd")` and `rbtdre_engine.rs` uses
`root_temp.join(case.name)`. There is no string-concatenation bug. The
failure occurs at a *substrate boundary*: a Windows-native binary
consuming Cygwin POSIX paths (`/cygdrive/c/...`) and combining them
with Windows-native separators via `PathBuf::join`. Windows path
normalization of the result is implementation-defined for these inputs,
which is why behavior varies between runs.

### Three boundary classes (scope estimate if revisited)

For a future attempt at making theurge work under Cygwin without
abandoning the Windows-native target, three boundary classes need
systematic handling:

1. **Env-var intake from Cygwin bash → Rust.** Single chokepoint:
   `rbtdb_read_dispatch_dir` in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` reads
   `BURD_TEMP_DIR`, `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`, etc. as POSIX strings. A
   conversion helper (e.g., `cygpath -w` equivalent) would convert
   incoming paths to Windows-native `PathBuf` at this single site.
2. **Script-embedded paths from Rust → Cygwin bash.** The chokepoint
   is `rbtdrf_run_bash` in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` (called
   ~11 times in that file alone), but the *interpolation sites* are
   the callers that build the script string via
   `format!("source '{}'", path.display())`. Every such site needs to
   replace `path.display()` with a POSIX-form conversion. Discovery
   recipe: `grep -rn "source '{}'" Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` plus adjacent
   `format!()` blocks. ~9-15 sites in `rbtdrf_fast.rs`; the
   `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` sibling may have more.
3. **Env vars on tabtarget spawn.** `Command::new(&tt)` sites in
   `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, `rbtdrf_handbook.rs`, `rbtdri_invocation.rs`
   inherit the parent's env. If intake conversion (boundary 1) is
   applied, env vars need re-conversion back to POSIX before any
   tabtarget spawn, since tabtargets re-enter `bud_dispatch` and read
   POSIX paths.

Windows-native tool spawns (`Command::new("git")`, `"curl"`,
`"docker"`) need no conversion — Windows-form paths are what those
tools natively want.

### Unknown tail

Even with all three boundaries handled, BUK-side bash itself may have
Cygwin assumptions that aren't yet visible. `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` slash
trimming, `realpath` semantics, `readlink -f` behavior, and similar
patterns could surface as the next blocker. The mechanical theurge fix
unlocks visibility into the next layer; whether that layer is clean is
empirically unknown.

## Cygwin substrate, revisited 2026-05-19

Pace ₢BOAAM (path-transmute) landed at commit `ea2d04b` and was
verified on `cygwin@rocket`. The path-transmutation code itself is
correct, but two BUK-side / theurge-invocation blockers surface
behind it. This section retires the "Unknown tail" prediction with
empirical content.

### Path-transmutation is correct but doesn't engage by default

`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrx_platform.rs:45` keys Cygwin detection on
`std::env::var("OSTYPE").as_deref() == Ok("cygwin")`. The module
docstring (line 42) asserts *"the launching bash exports
`OSTYPE=cygwin`"* — empirically false. Bash sets `OSTYPE` as a
non-exported shell variable; `env | grep ^OSTYPE=` from a Cygwin
session returns nothing, and the Windows-native `rbtd.exe` spawned
by a tabtarget sees no such env var. `rbtdrx_is_cygwin()` returns
false, so every intake/outflow site in the module operates as
identity. The mixed-separator failure described in "Observed
failure mode" above reproduces unchanged on the landed code.

Forcing `export OSTYPE` in the parent shell before invoking
`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` exercises the transmutation. Same
suite, two runs:

```
default:        Trace dir: /cygdrive/c/Users/.../temp-...\rbtd
                                      (mixed-slash, no rbtd/ created)
export OSTYPE:  Trace dir: C:\Users\cygwin\projects\...\temp-...\rbtd
                                      (clean native, rbtd/ exists)
```

`grep -rn OSTYPE Tools/buk Tools/rbk/rbtd` finds zero export sites
in the launcher chain. Either the BUK launcher needs to learn
`export OSTYPE`, or `rbtdrx_is_cygwin()` needs a detection signal
that does propagate to child processes (e.g., `cygpath --version`
succeeding, or shelling out to `uname -o` returning `Cygwin`).

### `Command::new("bash")` finds Windows WSL stub, not Cygwin bash

With OSTYPE forcibly exported, path-transmutation produces clean
native trace paths and the per-case directory is created. The
first case (`rbtdrf_ev_string_valid`) then fails with UTF-16LE
stderr from `wsl.exe`:

```
Windows Subsystem for Linux has no installed distributions.
```

`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs:68` calls
`Command::new("bash").arg("-c").arg(script)`. From a Windows-
native process, `CreateProcessW` uses the standard search order:
application directory, current directory, *system directory
(`C:\Windows\System32`)*, Windows directory, then `PATH`.
System32 ships `bash.exe` — the WSL launcher stub installed by
default on Windows 10+. Cygwin's `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe`
appears later via `PATH` and is never reached.

`cmd.exe /c where bash` from a Cygwin shell returns
`C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe` first because `where` performs a
`PATH`-only lookup; CreateProcessW's broader search is what trips
the Rust call. Reproduced with a 10-line standalone Rust binary
independent of theurge.

Fix path (out of scope for ₢BOAAM): resolve the bash absolute
path once at startup (e.g., via `cygpath -w /usr/bin/bash` →
`C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe`) and pass it to `Command::new`, rather
than relying on lookup order. Affects `rbtdrf_run_bash` and any
sibling site that spawns `bash` (or other Cygwin utilities whose
name collides with a System32 binary).

### Done condition for ₢BOAAM, literal vs effective

The pace's Done condition was *"`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh`
reaches a verdict on Cygwin."* Literally: met — the binary
terminates with exit 1 and emits `0 passed, 1 failed (1 total)`.
Effectively: the suite exercises only 1 of 75 cases before the
bash-lookup blocker stops it, which is not a transmutation gap.
The transmutation code does what it claims when its detection
signal is present.

### rustup install prerequisites on Cygwin

Standing up a fresh `cygwin@rocket` → x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
rustup install. Three gotchas not in the rustup docs:

1. **No MinGW system install needed.** Rust 1.95.0's
   `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain ships a self-contained
   linker. `cargo build` on theurge linked clean without
   `mingw64-x86_64-gcc-core` or any `setup-x86_64.exe -P`
   step. This eliminates the elevation barrier — the cygwin
   user does not need admin to install rust.

2. **`TMPDIR` must be a Windows-form path.** PATH on rocket
   resolves `curl` to `C:\WINDOWS\system32\curl.exe` (Schannel-
   backed Windows curl), not Cygwin's curl. rustup-init.sh's
   bootstrap downloader hands curl a Cygwin POSIX `/tmp/...`
   destination; Windows curl writes ~5KB then fails with
   `curl: (23) client returned ERROR on write` (curl error 23
   is "write failed"). Workaround:

   ```
   mkdir -p ~/rust-install-tmp
   export TMPDIR="$(cygpath -w ~/rust-install-tmp)"
   sh rustup-init.sh -y --default-toolchain stable \
       --profile minimal --no-modify-path
   ```

3. **Do not pass `--default-host`.** The rustup-init.sh wrapper
   detects Cygwin via uname and inserts its own
   `--default-host x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` before invoking the
   downloaded rustup-init.exe. Passing the flag again from the
   command line produces:

   ```
   error: the argument '--default-host <DEFAULT_HOST>' cannot
   be used multiple times
   ```

   Let the wrapper auto-detect; the installer line
   `info: setting default host triple to x86_64-pc-windows-gnu`
   confirms the correct host was chosen.

### SSH transport detail for cygwin@rocket

`memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`'s
noninteractive forced-command for `cygwin@rocket` is
`bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. Empirically, argv-mode
commands containing `$var` references arrive at the remote bash
without parameter expansion:

```
$ ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo $OSTYPE'
$OSTYPE                                  # literal, not 'cygwin'
$ printf '%s\n' 'echo $OSTYPE' | ssh cygwin@rocket bash -l
cygwin                                   # correct
```

Cause unconfirmed (the forced-command's shell quoting interacts
with how sshd reconstructs `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` through Windows
OpenSSH's argv-to-string join); the empirical pattern is
reliable. For noninteractive automation against `cygwin@rocket`,
use the stdin-pipe form. Argv-mode is acceptable for commands
that contain no `$` expansion and no operator chaining.

## The WSL substrate, today

### Observed result

A fresh Ubuntu-24.04 distro provisioned on the same Windows host, with
rustup installed and the repo cloned into ext4, ran the fast suite as
follows:

- Theurge built cleanly (`Finished dev profile target(s) in 4.27s`).
- `enrollment-validation`: **47/47 passed** — the exact fixture that
  could not complete a single case on Cygwin.
- `regime-validation`: 24/25 passed. The one failure
  (`rbtdrf_rv_rbrr_repo`) emitted
  `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX: RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX must not be empty` — a
  config-gap in the clone-of-clone chain (Mac → fresh Cygwin checkout
  → WSL clone) that would surface identically on any never-configured
  checkout regardless of OS.

Substrate-attributable cases: 71/71 passed. No mixed-separator
artifacts; trace directories landed where the Rust binary said they
would.

### Setup prerequisites observed

- `wsl --install -d Ubuntu-24.04 --no-launch` — see operational traps
  below; `--no-launch` is load-bearing for remote invocation.
- `apt-get install -y build-essential` — rustup warns "no default
  linker (`cc`) was found in your PATH" after a minimal-profile
  install; theurge builds C-using crates (e.g., libc build script,
  typenum, zerocopy) and fails to link without `cc`.
- A station regime file at `../station-files/burs.env` — BUK's
  first-run gate. The error message enumerates the three required
  variables (`BURS_LOG_DIR`, `BURS_USER`, `BURS_TINCTURE`).

## Operational traps when driving WSL from another machine

These applied when driving the Windows host from a Mac through the
existing `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh` chain (Mac bash → ssh →
Windows OpenSSH → cmd.exe → PowerShell → wsl.exe → bash). They are
specific to that transit, not to WSL itself.

### `wsl --install` over SSH

A bare `wsl --install -d <distro>` from a privileged SSH session
fails with

```
The requested operation requires elevation.
This operation requires an interactive window station.
Error code: Wsl/0x800705b3
```

The elevation part is solved by being on the privileged SSH path. The
window-station part is the post-install distro launch, which wants a
console for the first-time user-creation prompt. **Fix**: append
`--no-launch`. The distro installs successfully and remains in
"Stopped" state with `root` as the only account.

### `wsl.exe` UTF-16LE output

`wsl -l -v` (and `wsl --status` and friends) emit UTF-16LE directly.
Through cmd-mode SSH transport, the interleaved null bytes terminate
C-string handling and the output appears empty. **Fix**: invoke
through a PowerShell wrapper (PowerShell handles UTF-16 natively as
its console encoding) or set `WSL_UTF8=1` in the environment before
invoking wsl.

### PowerShell 5.1 quote-stripping

```
powershell -Command 'wsl -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "cd /path && tt/foo.sh"'
```

fails with

```
The token '&&' is not a valid statement separator in this version.
```

The cmd.exe → PowerShell argv shuffle strips the inner double quotes
around the `bash -lc` argument. PowerShell then receives the bash
command unquoted, parses `&&` as a PowerShell operator, and rejects it
(PS 5.1 has no `&&`; PS 7+ has it as a pipeline chain operator,
which still doesn't match the bash semantic we wanted). **Fix**: use
`wsl --cd <dir>` to set the working directory natively in WSL, then
invoke a single command without a `cd && ...` chain:

```
powershell -Command 'wsl -d <distro> -u root --cd /root/proj -- bash -lc tt/foo.sh'
```

### `/mnt/c` local-clone

The Windows filesystem is mounted at `/mnt/c/` inside WSL. Cygwin's
existing checkout at `/cygdrive/c/Users/<user>/projects/<repo>` is
therefore reachable from WSL as
`/mnt/c/Users/<user>/projects/<repo>`. Running tabtargets directly
from that mount surfaces FS-metadata problems (executable bits,
filename case, performance under high-fanout test fixtures).
**Workaround**:

```
wsl -d <distro> -u root -- git clone /mnt/c/Users/<user>/projects/<repo> /root/projects/<repo>
```

A local clone copies into ext4 cleanly, and tests run from the WSL
home directory rather than the Windows mount. The same trick lets you
copy single files across (e.g., `cp /mnt/c/.../station-files/burs.env
/root/.../station-files/burs.env`) without needing a separate
network transport.

## Live work captured in ₣BO

Two paces in heat ₣BO carry the substrate work forward:

- **₢BOAAM (cygwin-theurge-path-transmute)** — landed at commit
  `ea2d04b` on 2026-05-19, verified on `cygwin@rocket`. The
  path-transmutation code itself is correct, but two follow-on
  BUK-side / theurge-invocation blockers surface behind it (OSTYPE
  not exported by bash so the detection signal never reaches the
  Windows-native binary; `Command::new("bash")` resolves to
  System32's WSL stub before Cygwin's bash via CreateProcessW
  search order). Detail in the "Cygwin substrate, revisited
  2026-05-19" section above.
- **₢BOAAN (cygwin-wsl-noninteractive-ssh-personas)** — set up
  `cygwin@rocket` and `wsl@rocket` as parallel headless SSH accounts,
  each forced-command'd to one substrate. Independent of the
  substrate-viability question; useful for either substrate, since it
  collapses the multi-step transit chain in this memo's "Operational
  traps" section to a single `ssh wsl@rocket <cmd>` or
  `ssh cygwin@rocket <cmd>` invocation.

Both paces remain live. This memo records what we know, not which
substrate to invest in next.

## See also

- `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` — SSH
  account auth chain (recipe for both `cygwin@rocket` and
  `wsl@rocket` setup); the "Noninteractive forced-command variants"
  section covers the noninteractive personas and the WSL per-user
  registration finding (`wsl --install` needs admin; `wsl --import`
  does not)
- `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` and the
  `memo-2026051*-windows-transport-*` series — broader empirical
  record of cmd.exe / PowerShell / wsl.exe transport quoting; the
  trap in this memo's PowerShell-quote-stripping section is one
  instance of that family
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md` — Windows
  scripting guide; the codified body-authoring rules for transit
  through cmd.exe → wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell stacks
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` and `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`
  — chokepoints for the three boundary classes in the Cygwin section
- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` — `bujb_privileged_ssh` (the
  underpinning of `tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh`)

content.content:
1	# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-16
4	
5	## Why this memo exists
6	
7	When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
8	but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
9	"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
10	`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
11	failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
12	(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
13	investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
14	Log spelunking.
15	
16	The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
17	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
18	operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
19	The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.
20	
21	## The chain, in causal order
22	
23	### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile
24	
25	```
26	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
27	```
28	
29	Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
30	does NOT create:
31	
32	- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
33	- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`
34	
35	The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
36	explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
37	alone does NOT trigger profile creation.
38	
39	### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile
40	
41	The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
42	— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
43	`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
44	`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.
45	
46	For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
47	sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
48	(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
49	with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
50	the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
51	with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
52	because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
53	was attempted.
54	
55	### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup
56	
57	The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
58	path:
59	
60	```
61	Match User <name>
62	    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/authorized_keys
63	```
64	
65	`__PROGRAMDATA__` is sshd's token for `C:\ProgramData`. The
66	absolute path means sshd never asks the OS where the home is — it
67	opens the file directly. This is Microsoft's documented pattern for
68	service-style accounts.
69	
70	This is the design choice in `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
71	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125`). It is not a Cygwin or
72	Windows path organization preference — it is structurally required
73	for any account that will never have an interactive profile. The
74	spec text presents the Match block as a routing detail; the
75	underlying reason is the `GetUserProfileDirectoryW` chain.
76	
77	### 4. Orphan `C:\Users\<name>` causes silent `.machinename` profile suffix
78	
79	If `C:\Users\<name>` exists as a directory at the moment a Windows
80	session first creates the account's profile, User Profile Service
81	disambiguates by minting `C:\Users\<name>.<machinename>` instead.
82	The original directory becomes an orphan.
83	
84	This bit during the brad@rocket setup: a pre-existing
85	`C:\Users\brad\` from an earlier failed
86	`%h/.ssh/authorized_keys` attempt caused brad's actual profile to
87	land at `C:\Users\brad.rocket\`.
88	
89	Operational consequence for `bujuw_user`: garrison's destructive
90	cleanup must remove both `C:\Users\<workload>` and any
91	`C:\Users\<workload>.*` siblings before the next profile creation.
92	A residual orphan silently splits the workload's home for the next
93	garrison cycle.
94	
95	### 5. Cygwin and Windows disagree on "home" for the same user
96	
97	After profile creation, two different APIs return two different
98	home paths:
99	
100	| Caller                  | Returns for brad on rocket           |
101	|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
102	| `getpwnam("brad")`      | `/home/brad` (POSIX default)         |
103	| `$HOME` in shell        | `/cygdrive/c/Users/brad.rocket`      |
104	| `~brad` (tilde expand)  | `/home/brad`                         |
105	
106	SSH tools (ssh, ssh-keygen, scp, git-over-ssh) use `getpwnam` and
107	land on `/home/brad`. Login bash inherits `$HOME` from the Windows
108	session and lands on the Windows profile path. They disagree.
109	
110	Symptoms during the brad setup: `mkdir -p ~/.ssh` succeeded but
111	ssh-keygen with default path still failed because `~` and
112	ssh-keygen's path resolution diverged.
113	
114	Mitigation for a target account: set `HOME=/home/<name>` in
115	`.bash_profile` and place `.ssh/` there. SSH tools and bash then
116	agree on a single home.
117	
118	This concern applies to `bujuw_user` too. Worth a directed probe
119	during practice runs:
120	
121	```
122	ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'
123	```
124	
125	If split-HOME is observed, the workload's planted privkey at
126	`z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable by `git push` (which
127	uses `$HOME` expansion). If login bash already auto-aligns via
128	`/etc/profile`, no action needed.
129	
130	### 6. StrictModes ACL allowlist
131	
132	sshd's strict-mode check on authkeys files allows ACL entries only
133	for: the user, SYSTEM, BUILTIN\Administrators. Any other principal
134	in the ACL causes silent refusal — even read-only entries like
135	`Everyone:(RX)` or `BUILTIN\Users:(RX)`.
136	
137	Inherited ACLs from `C:\Users\` and `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\`
138	include these by default. Explicit lockdown is required:
139	
140	```
141	icacls C:\Path\To\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F <user>:R
142	```
143	
144	Owner must be the user OR a member of Administrators. Both are
145	accepted.
146	
147	## Worked example: stand up a parallel test account
148	
149	(brad@rocket, 2026-05-16. Substitute names as needed.)
150	
151	Admin SSH session on rocket:
152	
153	```
154	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
155	mkdir C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad
156	# Write authorized_keys content: command="<shell-launch>" <pubkey>
157	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F brad:R
158	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F
159	# Append Match block to C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config:
160	#   Match User brad
161	#       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys
162	sshd.exe -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config   # validate
163	Restart-Service sshd
164	```
165	
166	From any client whose private key matches the pubkey:
167	
168	```
169	ssh brad@rocket
170	```
171	
172	Inside the new session, align HOME to avoid the Cygwin split:
173	
174	```
175	mkdir -p /home/brad/.ssh
176	chmod 700 /home/brad/.ssh
177	printf 'export HOME=/home/brad\ncd "$HOME"\n' > /home/brad/.bash_profile
178	```
179	
180	## Noninteractive forced-command variants
181	
182	The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
183	Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
184	access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
185	The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
186	authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
187	substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
188	substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
189	brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:
190	
191	- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
192	- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`
193	
194	The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.
195	
196	### Forced-command pattern
197	
198	Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:
199	
200	```
201	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
202	```
203	
204	The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
205	command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
206	variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.
207	
208	**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
209	login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:
210	
211	```
212	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
213	```
214	
215	The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
216	inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
217	spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
218	is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
219	body and executes whatever the client supplied.
220	
221	**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
222	authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
223	(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
224	cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
225	`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
226	runtime concern:
227	
228	```
229	@echo off
230	set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
231	C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
232	```
233	
234	The authkeys forced-command then becomes:
235	
236	```
237	command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
238	```
239	
240	The wrapper does two load-bearing things:
241	
242	- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
243	  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
244	  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
245	  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
246	  declaration.
247	- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
248	  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
249	  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
250	  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.
251	
252	The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
253	(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).
254	
255	### Per-user WSL state and provisioning without elevation
256	
257	WSL distros are registered per Windows user under
258	`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`. A distro
259	installed by user A is not visible to user B. The headless `wsl`
260	Windows account therefore needs its own distro registration — but
261	`wsl --install`, the obvious provisioning command, requires admin
262	elevation (it enables the WSL Windows feature, which is system-wide)
263	and the wsl account is not (and should not be) an admin.
264	
265	`wsl --import` works around this. It registers an existing distro
266	tarball into the *current* user's `Lxss` registry without elevation —
267	because the WSL Windows feature is already enabled at the system level,
268	and `--import` only adds the per-user registration. The bootstrap
269	pattern:
270	
271	1. As an admin user that already has the distro installed:
272	   ```
273	   wsl --export Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar
274	   ```
275	2. Grant the headless user read on the tarball and modify on the install
276	   location:
277	   ```
278	   icacls C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar /grant wsl:R
279	   icacls C:\WSL /grant 'wsl:(OI)(CI)M'
280	   ```
281	3. Temporarily set the headless user's authkeys forced-command to a
282	   one-shot import:
283	   ```
284	   command="C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe --import Ubuntu-24.04 C:\WSL\Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar"
285	   ```
286	4. Trigger from the SSH client — any command fires the forced-command
287	   and the connection blocks until import completes:
288	   ```
289	   ssh wsl@host trigger
290	   ```
291	5. Restore the runtime forced-command (the `runcmd.cmd`-based variant
292	   above), re-lock its ACL, and delete the tarball.
293	
294	The wsl account installs its own WSL state through its own SSH session,
295	because `--import` does not need the rights that `--install` does.
296	Principle-clean: the registration runs as the target user, no rights
297	modifications on the headless account, no cross-user impersonation.
298	
299	### Operational notes
300	
301	**ACL re-lock after `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`.** Writing a fresh file
302	via the .NET method resets the file's ACL to inherited from its parent
303	directory, undoing the `icacls /inheritance:r` lockdown established in
304	section 6 above. Re-apply the icacls lockdown after every rewrite of
305	authkeys (or the wrapper `.cmd`, or any file living under the locked
306	ProgramData subtree). `Set-Content` has the same behavior. The sequence
307	is: rewrite file → reapply ACL.
308	
309	**Transit content past cmd.exe / PowerShell.** Writing arbitrary file
310	content from a remote bash session over `ssh ... cmd.exe ... powershell
311	... wsl.exe` is a quoting graveyard — `&&`, `|`, `$VAR`, `\"`, and `'`
312	all behave differently at each layer. The reliable transit is to
313	base64-encode content on the curia side and decode in PowerShell:
314	
315	```
316	B64=$(printf '%s\n' "$CONTENT" | openssl enc -base64 -A)
317	PS_CMD="[IO.File]::WriteAllText('C:\\path\\file', [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('${B64}')))"
318	ENCODED=$(printf '%s' "$PS_CMD" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-16LE | openssl enc -base64 -A)
319	ssh user@host powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand "$ENCODED"
320	```
321	
322	The two base64 layers protect different segments: the inner one keeps
323	file content opaque to PowerShell's parser; the outer one
324	(`-EncodedCommand`) keeps the entire PowerShell command opaque to
325	cmd.exe's tokenizer. The matching code in this project's bash utilities
326	uses `openssl enc -base64 -A` consistently — see
327	`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` for prior art in the same shape.
328	
329	**`wsl.exe` emits UTF-16LE.** Output from `wsl -l -v`, `wsl --status`,
330	and `wsl --install` is UTF-16LE on stdout. Through cmd-mode SSH
331	transport the interleaved null bytes terminate C-string handling and
332	the output appears empty or garbled (single visible characters spaced
333	out). Force UTF-8 with `$env:WSL_UTF8=1` set before the wsl invocation,
334	or invoke via a PowerShell wrapper that handles UTF-16 natively.
335	
336	## See also
337	
338	- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125` — `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
339	- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:166-168` — `BUJB_command_b/c/w` forced-command directives
340	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc` — caparison spec
341	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGC-GarrisonCygwin.adoc` — garrison-c spec
342	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` — garrison-w spec
343	- Microsoft OpenSSH for Windows StrictModes documentation
344

file.content:
# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy

Date: 2026-05-16

## Why this memo exists

When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
Log spelunking.

The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.

## The chain, in causal order

### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile

```
net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
```

Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
does NOT create:

- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`

The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
alone does NOT trigger profile creation.

### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile

The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.

For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
was attempted.

### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup

The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
path:

```
Match User <name>
    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/authorized_keys
```

`__PROGRAMDATA__` is sshd's token for `C:\ProgramData`. The
absolute path means sshd never asks the OS where the home is — it
opens the file directly. This is Microsoft's documented pattern for
service-style accounts.

This is the design choice in `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125`). It is not a Cygwin or
Windows path organization preference — it is structurally required
for any account that will never have an interactive profile. The
spec text presents the Match block as a routing detail; the
underlying reason is the `GetUserProfileDirectoryW` chain.

### 4. Orphan `C:\Users\<name>` causes silent `.machinename` profile suffix

If `C:\Users\<name>` exists as a directory at the moment a Windows
session first creates the account's profile, User Profile Service
disambiguates by minting `C:\Users\<name>.<machinename>` instead.
The original directory becomes an orphan.

This bit during the brad@rocket setup: a pre-existing
`C:\Users\brad\` from an earlier failed
`%h/.ssh/authorized_keys` attempt caused brad's actual profile to
land at `C:\Users\brad.rocket\`.

Operational consequence for `bujuw_user`: garrison's destructive
cleanup must remove both `C:\Users\<workload>` and any
`C:\Users\<workload>.*` siblings before the next profile creation.
A residual orphan silently splits the workload's home for the next
garrison cycle.

### 5. Cygwin and Windows disagree on "home" for the same user

After profile creation, two different APIs return two different
home paths:

| Caller                  | Returns for brad on rocket           |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| `getpwnam("brad")`      | `/home/brad` (POSIX default)         |
| `$HOME` in shell        | `/cygdrive/c/Users/brad.rocket`      |
| `~brad` (tilde expand)  | `/home/brad`                         |

SSH tools (ssh, ssh-keygen, scp, git-over-ssh) use `getpwnam` and
land on `/home/brad`. Login bash inherits `$HOME` from the Windows
session and lands on the Windows profile path. They disagree.

Symptoms during the brad setup: `mkdir -p ~/.ssh` succeeded but
ssh-keygen with default path still failed because `~` and
ssh-keygen's path resolution diverged.

Mitigation for a target account: set `HOME=/home/<name>` in
`.bash_profile` and place `.ssh/` there. SSH tools and bash then
agree on a single home.

This concern applies to `bujuw_user` too. Worth a directed probe
during practice runs:

```
ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'
```

If split-HOME is observed, the workload's planted privkey at
`z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable by `git push` (which
uses `$HOME` expansion). If login bash already auto-aligns via
`/etc/profile`, no action needed.

### 6. StrictModes ACL allowlist

sshd's strict-mode check on authkeys files allows ACL entries only
for: the user, SYSTEM, BUILTIN\Administrators. Any other principal
in the ACL causes silent refusal — even read-only entries like
`Everyone:(RX)` or `BUILTIN\Users:(RX)`.

Inherited ACLs from `C:\Users\` and `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\`
include these by default. Explicit lockdown is required:

```
icacls C:\Path\To\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F <user>:R
```

Owner must be the user OR a member of Administrators. Both are
accepted.

## Worked example: stand up a parallel test account

(brad@rocket, 2026-05-16. Substitute names as needed.)

Admin SSH session on rocket:

```
net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
mkdir C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad
# Write authorized_keys content: command="<shell-launch>" <pubkey>
icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F brad:R
icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F
# Append Match block to C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config:
#   Match User brad
#       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys
sshd.exe -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config   # validate
Restart-Service sshd
```

From any client whose private key matches the pubkey:

```
ssh brad@rocket
```

Inside the new session, align HOME to avoid the Cygwin split:

```
mkdir -p /home/brad/.ssh
chmod 700 /home/brad/.ssh
printf 'export HOME=/home/brad\ncd "$HOME"\n' > /home/brad/.bash_profile
```

## Noninteractive forced-command variants

The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:

- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`

The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.

### Forced-command pattern

Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
```

The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.

**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
```

The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
body and executes whatever the client supplied.

**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
runtime concern:

```
@echo off
set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The authkeys forced-command then becomes:

```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```

The wrapper does two load-bearing things:

- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
  declaration.
- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.

The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).

### Per-user WSL state and provisioning without elevation

WSL distros are registered per Windows user under
`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`. A distro
installed by user A is not visible to user B. The headless `wsl`
Windows account therefore needs its own distro registration — but
`wsl --install`, the obvious provisioning command, requires admin
elevation (it enables the WSL Windows feature, which is system-wide)
and the wsl account is not (and should not be) an admin.

`wsl --import` works around this. It registers an existing distro
tarball into the *current* user's `Lxss` registry without elevation —
because the WSL Windows feature is already enabled at the system level,
and `--import` only adds the per-user registration. The bootstrap
pattern:

1. As an admin user that already has the distro installed:
   ```
   wsl --export Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar
   ```
2. Grant the headless user read on the tarball and modify on the install
   location:
   ```
   icacls C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar /grant wsl:R
   icacls C:\WSL /grant 'wsl:(OI)(CI)M'
   ```
3. Temporarily set the headless user's authkeys forced-command to a
   one-shot import:
   ```
   command="C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe --import Ubuntu-24.04 C:\WSL\Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar"
   ```
4. Trigger from the SSH client — any command fires the forced-command
   and the connection blocks until import completes:
   ```
   ssh wsl@host trigger
   ```
5. Restore the runtime forced-command (the `runcmd.cmd`-based variant
   above), re-lock its ACL, and delete the tarball.

The wsl account installs its own WSL state through its own SSH session,
because `--import` does not need the rights that `--install` does.
Principle-clean: the registration runs as the target user, no rights
modifications on the headless account, no cross-user impersonation.

### Operational notes

**ACL re-lock after `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`.** Writing a fresh file
via the .NET method resets the file's ACL to inherited from its parent
directory, undoing the `icacls /inheritance:r` lockdown established in
section 6 above. Re-apply the icacls lockdown after every rewrite of
authkeys (or the wrapper `.cmd`, or any file living under the locked
ProgramData subtree). `Set-Content` has the same behavior. The sequence
is: rewrite file → reapply ACL.

**Transit content past cmd.exe / PowerShell.** Writing arbitrary file
content from a remote bash session over `ssh ... cmd.exe ... powershell
... wsl.exe` is a quoting graveyard — `&&`, `|`, `$VAR`, `\"`, and `'`
all behave differently at each layer. The reliable transit is to
base64-encode content on the curia side and decode in PowerShell:

```
B64=$(printf '%s\n' "$CONTENT" | openssl enc -base64 -A)
PS_CMD="[IO.File]::WriteAllText('C:\\path\\file', [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('${B64}')))"
ENCODED=$(printf '%s' "$PS_CMD" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-16LE | openssl enc -base64 -A)
ssh user@host powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand "$ENCODED"
```

The two base64 layers protect different segments: the inner one keeps
file content opaque to PowerShell's parser; the outer one
(`-EncodedCommand`) keeps the entire PowerShell command opaque to
cmd.exe's tokenizer. The matching code in this project's bash utilities
uses `openssl enc -base64 -A` consistently — see
`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` for prior art in the same shape.

**`wsl.exe` emits UTF-16LE.** Output from `wsl -l -v`, `wsl --status`,
and `wsl --install` is UTF-16LE on stdout. Through cmd-mode SSH
transport the interleaved null bytes terminate C-string handling and
the output appears empty or garbled (single visible characters spaced
out). Force UTF-8 with `$env:WSL_UTF8=1` set before the wsl invocation,
or invoke via a PowerShell wrapper that handles UTF-16 natively.

## See also

- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125` — `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:166-168` — `BUJB_command_b/c/w` forced-command directives
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc` — caparison spec
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGC-GarrisonCygwin.adoc` — garrison-c spec
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` — garrison-w spec
- Microsoft OpenSSH for Windows StrictModes documentation

1	# Windows hive cleanup — reboot-as-canonical-primitive decision
2	
3	Captured during pace ₢A-ABG (rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures /
4	windows-setup-first-time-debug) on 2026-05-11. This memo preserves the
5	design exploration that landed on **unconditional host reboot** as the
6	canonical Windows state-reset primitive for destructive cleanup, so
7	future maintainers can re-derive (or revise) the choice without
8	re-discovering.
9	
10	## Symptom
11	
12	On bujn-winpc, garrison-w's obliterate Phase 2 `Remove-CimInstance` on
13	`Win32_UserProfile` failed with:
14	
15	> The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another
16	> process.
17	> HRESULT 0x80041017 (WBEM_E_INVALID_QUERY in the WQL probe;
18	> file-locked Win32 error in the destructive call).
19	
20	Triage surfaced two stacked bugs:
21	
22	1. **WQL backslash-escape bug.** The probe filter `LocalPath = 'C:\Users\<user>'`
23	   bombed at the WMI provider because WQL treats `\` as the literal-escape
24	   character in string literals; the path needs `\\` doubling. The
25	   bash-side `|| z_profiles_raw=""` fallback silently absorbed the
26	   provider error and downgraded "query failed" to "no rows," yielding
27	   *false-positive garrison-w success* while leaving six accumulating
28	   Win32_UserProfile rows (canonical + five `.rocket.NNN` demotion
29	   fallbacks).
30	
31	2. **Hive-stuck race.** Even after fixing the WQL escape so the probe
32	   correctly returned the canonical row, `Remove-CimInstance` itself
33	   failed because the workload's NTUSER.DAT was still mounted under
34	   `HKU\<workload-SID>` (and `HKU\<workload-SID>_Classes`). The SSH-as-
35	   workload sessions garrison-w runs (`[w-session-1/4]` through
36	   `[w-session-4/4]`) cause UserProfileSvc to mount the hive on logon;
37	   on logoff, UserProfileSvc *eventually* unmounts but the race window
38	   between session end and unmount is large enough to hit destructive
39	   admin operations.
40	
41	## Websearch — prior art
42	
43	The phenomenon is well-known under the canonical observable **Event ID
44	1552** ("User hive is loaded by another process (Registry Lock)").
45	Microsoft historically shipped a separate tool **UPHClean** ("User
46	Profile Hive Cleanup Service") that monitored for logged-off users with
47	still-loaded hives and forcibly released holding handles; post-Vista,
48	UPHClean was integrated into UserProfileSvc with its own retry timer.
49	Industry-named contributing causes: antivirus scanners, Windows Fast
50	Startup, helper-process races (in our case WSL helpers `wslhost` /
51	`wslservice` / `vmwp` briefly holding handles to HKCU\Lxss subkeys).
52	Standard remedies in escalation order: wait/retry, manual
53	`reg unload HKU\<SID>`, `RUNDLL32 advapi32.dll,ProcessIdleTasks`,
54	reboot, safe-mode delete.
55	
56	## Levels considered
57	
58	A four-tier mitigation ladder mapped to escalating aggressiveness:
59	
60	| Tier | Mechanism | Reliability | Cost |
61	|---|---|---|---|
62	| 1 | `reg unload HKU\<SID>_Classes` then `HKU\<SID>` before Remove-CimInstance, gated on `Loaded=True` probe | ~80% (fails when non-WSL holders — Defender, Search indexer, vmwp — hold the hive) | ~40 LOC + WQL fix |
63	| 2 | Tier 1 + enumerate open handles via P/Invoke to `NtQuerySystemInformation`/`SystemHandleInformation`, kill non-essential PIDs holding workload-related handles | ~98% (UserProfileSvc itself remains possible holder) | ~150 LOC + empirical filter tuning |
64	| 3 | Tier 2 + `Stop-Service profsvc -Force; Start-Service profsvc` | ~99.5% (collateral risk on shared hosts) | ~30 LOC additional |
65	| 4 | Reboot (`Restart-Computer -Force`) | **100%** as long as the host boots | ~30 LOC |
66	
67	A separate "Level 3 redesign" was explored: admin pre-creates the
68	workload profile via `CreateProfile` P/Invoke (userenv.dll), `wsl --import`
69	runs as admin, the vhdx is moved to a workload-owned path, admin deletes
70	its own HKCU\Lxss entry via raw registry (not `wsl --unregister`, which
71	would delete the vhdx), and the Lxss subtree is offline-injected into
72	workload's NTUSER.DAT via `reg load` / `reg unload`. **This was rejected
73	as a race fix** because the first real workload SSH use (the entire
74	point of having a workload account) re-creates the hive mount and
75	re-opens the race window. Shifting *when* the first hive mount occurs
76	(from setup to first use) doesn't eliminate the race. The
77	architecture's *independent* value (smaller code, no SSH-as-workload
78	during setup) is captured in the admin-no-WSL paddock discussion (see
79	pace ₢A-ABJ as reframed).
80	
81	## Decision: Tier 4 (reboot) as the primitive
82	
83	Empirically validated on bujn-winpc: pre-reboot showed workload SID
84	`S-1-5-21-…-1029` + `_Classes` mounted under HKU with `Loaded=True`;
85	post-reboot (uptime delta 15h → 48s, ~14s SSH return; second-cycle
86	~42s) all hives unmounted, all six demoted Win32_UserProfile rows
87	cleanly removable. Garrison-w converges end-to-end with reboot as
88	prelude.
89	
90	**Rationale for picking Tier 4 over Tier 1/2/3:**
91	
92	- **Determinism.** Tiers 1-3 are probabilistic; Tier 4 is binary
93	  (it works or the host won't boot).
94	- **No undocumented internals.** Tiers 2-3 depend on
95	  `NtQuerySystemInformation` handle enumeration, UserProfileSvc
96	  internals, and empirical tuning of process-kill exclusion lists.
97	  Tier 4 depends only on `Restart-Computer -Force` and SSH
98	  reachability — both first-class, stable interfaces.
99	- **Code surface.** Tier 4 is the smallest (~30 LOC for the helper)
100	  despite being the most reliable. Tiers 1-3 grew code without
101	  matching reliability gains.
102	- **Diagnostic surface.** A failed reboot is a single, well-
103	  understood failure mode (Group Policy block, pending operations,
104	  hardware refusing to boot). A failed Tier 2 handle-hunt requires
105	  bespoke debugging.
106	- **Cost.** ~30-60s per garrison invocation in our deployment (modern
107	  hardware, NVMe SSD). Garrison-w is a multi-minute operation; the
108	  reboot cost is rounding error.
109	
110	**The reboot helper's load-bearing preconditions** (all delivered by
111	caparison-windows; cross-referenced in BUSJCW):
112	
113	- sshd is a Windows service starting at boot.
114	- Tailscale service `StartType = Automatic` (BURN_HOST resolves on
115	  Tailnet after reboot without operator action).
116	- `administrators_authorized_keys` + sshd_config Match block persist
117	  across reboot (they live in `C:\ProgramData\ssh`).
118	
119	## Note: Windows Fast Startup is not what we observed — and we bypass it explicitly anyway
120	
121	It's tempting to attribute the ~14-42s reboot times to Windows Fast
122	Startup, but **Fast Startup applies to `shutdown` (hybrid shutdown
123	saving the kernel session), not `restart`**. `Restart-Computer` /
124	`shutdown /r` does a full cold boot per documented PowerShell semantics.
125	The fast wall-clock times we saw are modern UEFI fast boot + NVMe SSD
126	performance, not OS-level fast startup. The polling-cap rationale
127	(`BUJB_reboot_poll_cap_s='600'`, 10 minutes) is dominated by the
128	pending-Windows-update worst case, not by normal boot variance.
129	
130	**Belt-and-suspenders explicit cold restart.** The helper dispatches
131	`shutdown.exe /full /r /f /t 0` rather than `Restart-Computer -Force`.
132	The `/full` flag is the documented hatch to bypass Fast Startup
133	behavior; while redundant on `/r` (restart) under current Windows
134	semantics, it makes the intent visible in code and is robust to any
135	future MS default changes. We are not relying on "restart happens to
136	do a cold boot by default" — we are asking for a cold boot explicitly.
137	
138	## Implementation pointers
139	
140	- Helper: `zbujb_reboot_and_await_ssh` in
141	  `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`.
142	- Wired unconditionally on `BURN_PLATFORM = bubep_windows` in
143	  `bujb_garrison()` between `bujp_preflight` and
144	  `zbujb_obliterate_workload`.
145	- Tinder constants: `BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_5`,
146	  `BUJB_reboot_poll_interval_s`, `BUJB_reboot_poll_cap_s`.
147	- Phase 2 obliterate simplified to WQL-escape + buc_die-on-probe-
148	  failure; no in-process unload remediation.
149	- Pace ₢A-ABG commits (`ff8d1e36`, `3fceebb3`, `086ab90d`,
150	  `08e26ec5`) carry the diff.
151	
152	## When to revisit this decision
153	
154	- If Windows ever ships a deterministic in-process hive-unload API,
155	  Tier 4 may stop being smallest.
156	- If we deploy to hosts where reboot is unacceptably invasive (shared
157	  workstations, long-running stateful Windows services) — currently
158	  out of scope per BUSJGW §Deferred.
159	- If pending Windows updates routinely extend reboot beyond 10
160	  minutes, we may need to raise `BUJB_reboot_poll_cap_s` or split the
161	  cap into "first-attempt cap" vs "update-install cap" with operator
162	  notification on the threshold cross.
163
